the_one_thing.statement: Scale of 5 means each symbol represents 5 items, and students can use skip-counting by 5s—a skill they already know—to find totals when reading and creating scaled graphs.

critical_misconception.label: Counting Symbols Instead of Scaling
critical_misconception.why_critical: Students ignore the scale value and count pictures directly. When scale says 'each symbol = 5' and there are 4 symbols, student answers '4' instead of '20.'

the_one_thing.success_indicator: Student correctly creates a picture graph where 20 items = 4 symbols AND can explain 'I counted by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20—that's 4 groups of 5.'
the_one_thing.biggest_risk: Students who don't connect skip-counting to symbol meaning will struggle when M4 introduces scale of 10 and requires reading values between tick marks on bar graphs.

student_facing_goal: You'll learn to make picture graphs where each symbol shows 5 items—not 2, but 5! You already know how to count by 5s. That same skill helps you read and create these graphs.
exit_check_tests: Can student create a scaled picture graph (1:5) from a data table?
Can student determine correct number of symbols for multiples of 5 (20 items = 4 symbols)?
Can student explain the connection between skip-counting and scale ('Each symbol is 5, so I count by 5s')?
Can student answer 'how many more/fewer' and 'in all' questions using their created graph?
Can student recognize that scale of 5 is more efficient than scale of 2 for larger numbers?
learning_goals: Represent data using scaled picture graphs.

Draw scaled picture graphs to represent a data set with several categories. Draw scaled bar graphs to represent a data set with several categories. Solve one- and two-step 'how many more' and 'how many fewer' problems using information presented in scaled bar graphs.

vocabulary_staging.approach: Use naturally during scale comparison; connect to M2: 'Last time you used groups of 2. Now groups of 5!' No formal definitions.
Explicitly formalize after grouping animation; 'This is a scale of 5—each symbol shows 5 items. Count by 5s to find totals.'
Introduce when reading/creating bar graphs: 'the numbers written along the side of the graph on the AXIS'; 'Read the HEIGHT—where the bar ENDS.'
Expect student use in varied contexts; brief reinforcement only
Expect natural student use; consolidate real-world connection and 'two scales in toolkit' framing

pre_completion_progression.section_1: 1 of 4 pre-filled (Swings from animation); student fills 3

bridge.from_module_2: Students learned the critical leap: each symbol can represent multiple items. They mastered scale of 2, including half-symbols for odd numbers. They understand that the KEY shows the SCALE and can create scaled picture graphs and solve comparison and total problems.
bridge.this_module: Students extend scaling understanding to scale of 5 through: connecting scale to skip-counting by 5s (a familiar Grade 2 skill); creating scaled picture graphs (1:5) with multiples of 5 only (no half-symbols); comparing efficiency of 1:2 vs 1:5 side-by-side (front-loaded in Warmup); introducing bar graph CREATION while keeping scale constant; three-scale toggle (1, 2, 5) in Interaction 3.2b.
bridge.to_module_4: Students will apply bar graph creation fluency to scale of 10. M3's bar graph introduction (same scale, new format) separates the cognitive shifts—students will already be comfortable with bar format before M4 adds scale of 10. Half-symbols return in M4 where half of 10 = 5.

core_concepts: Scale of 5: Each Symbol Represents 5 Items
Scale Efficiency: 1:5 Uses Fewer Symbols Than 1:2
Bar Graph FORMAT Introduction (Same Scale, New Skill)
Three-Scale Pattern: Bigger Scale = Fewer Symbols
Both Formats (Picture and Bar) Show Same Data

available_visuals.description: Picture Graphs (Mode 2: Creating) and Bar Graphs (Mode 2: Creating — NEW in M3) are the primary visuals. Bar graphs introduced mid-lesson (Section 2) alongside picture graphs. Grouping Animation moves to Lesson Section 1 in M3 (Warmup uses scale comparison instead). Data Tables use Numeric Display only throughout M3 (Symbol Display removed per SME feedback). Scale toggle interaction appears in Warmup (W.2 side-by-side) and Lesson (3.2b three-scale toggle).

numeric_display.description: Value column shows numbers only. Used exclusively throughout M3.
used_in: All phases — Warmup, all Lesson sections, Exit Check, Synthesis

visible_in: W.2 (data results after collection game — not displayed, data shown via graphs)
Lesson Section 1 (numeric display alongside creation tasks)
Lesson Section 2 (numeric display alongside bar graph creation)
Lesson Section 3 (numeric display — students create both formats independently)
Exit Check EC.1 and EC.2 (numeric display for graph creation)
hidden_in: Lesson 1.6 (students must read from graph alone)
Lesson 2.2 (reading bar height without table support)
Exit Check EC.3 (reading scaled graph without table)

constraints: 1:5 scale only throughout entire module — each symbol equals 5 items
All values are multiples of 5 (10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40) — no half-symbols
Grouping Animation appears in Lesson Section 1 (NOT Warmup — moves from M2 structure)
Warmup uses side-by-side 1:2 vs 1:5 comparison instead of grouping animation
Bar Graphs introduced in Lesson Section 2 only — always shown alongside picture graph first
Bar graph interaction is click-to-height (vertical) or click-to-width (horizontal) — NOT drag
Axis labeled in multiples of 5; all bars land exactly at tick marks (no interpolation)
Orientation: Sections 1-2 vertical; Section 3 horizontal (Guide explicitly acknowledges switch)
Three-scale toggle (Interaction 3.2b) is observation/exploration only — students do NOT create at scales 1 or 2
Half-symbols NOT available — not applicable when all values are multiples of 5
Symbol Display mode for data tables is NOT used in M3 (numeric only per SME feedback)

advanced_vocabulary: tick marks (as formal term — can point to them, do not name formally)
half-symbol (not applicable — all values multiples of 5)
advanced_concepts: Half-symbols or non-multiples of 5 (return in Module 4)
Bar graph interpolation — reading values between tick marks (Module 4)
Two-step problems as single submissions (Module 6 deep practice)

forbidden_phrases.phrase: This is called a BAR GRAPH
forbidden_phrases.reason: Students already know bar graphs from M1
Not applicable — all values multiples of 5
No interpolation — all values at tick marks

forbidden_phrases.phrase: You're so smart! / Perfect! / Amazing! / Excellent!

misconceptions.description: Students ignore the scale value and count pictures directly. Trigger behavior: when scale says 'each symbol = 5' and there are 4 symbols, student answers '4' instead of '20.' Student counts the pictures, ignoring the key/scale.
why_it_happens: Grade 2 experience was 1:1 (symbol = item); M2 introduced 1:2 but habit may not be solid
Natural inclination to count what you see
Larger scale (5 vs 2) makes the gap between symbol count and actual value bigger
Key/scale may be visually small or overlooked
prevention: 'Check the key first' routine: every graph reading starts with 'What does each symbol show?'
Scale comparison in Warmup makes impact visible (same data, fewer symbols with scale of 5)
Interaction 1.3 includes '4' as distractor (symbol count); Interaction 2.2 includes '3' as distractor
Reversal checks after creating: 'X symbols, each shows 5, that's Y'

remediation.light: Check the key. Each symbol shows how many? Now count by 5s.
remediation.medium: Return to grouping animation; show 4 symbols unpacking to 20 items
remediation.heavy: Side-by-side: same 20 items shown as 10 symbols (1:2) vs 4 symbols (1:5)

misconceptions.description: For 'How many more chose A than B?' student adds values instead of subtracting. More likely with larger numbers (25 + 35 = 60 instead of 35 - 25 = 10). Trigger behavior: word 'more' triggers addition.
why_it_happens: Larger numbers in M3 (vs M1-M2) make mental math harder, increasing errors
Students may not have solidified M1's teaching that 'how many more' = compare = subtract
prevention: Explicit callback: 'Remember from before: how many MORE means COMPARE. We find the DIFFERENCE.'
Language consistency: always pair 'how many more/fewer' with 'find the difference' and 'subtract'
Visual support: highlight both bars/symbols being compared before asking the question
Interaction 2.6 includes '45' as distractor (sum instead of difference)

remediation.light: This is a comparison question. Compare means find the difference. Subtract.
remediation.medium: Visually show the difference — gap between bars or symbol counts
remediation.heavy: Return to concrete: 'A has 35, B has 25. If B got 10 MORE, they'd be equal. So A has 10 more.'

misconceptions.description: With 'each symbol = 5' and 4 symbols shown, student calculates 4+5=9 instead of counting by 5s to get 20. Trigger behavior: student treats scale value as a number to add rather than a multiplier.
why_it_happens: Addition is the dominant operation students know
'Each symbol = 5' might be interpreted as 'add 5 to the count'
prevention: 'Groups of' language: '4 symbols means 4 groups of 5'
Skip-counting as bridge: 'Count by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20. That's what 4 groups of 5 gives you.'

remediation.medium: Animate symbols expanding to show items; count all items
remediation.heavy: Use hands: 'Each hand has 5 fingers. 4 hands = 5, 10, 15, 20 fingers.'

phases.purpose: Activate prior knowledge of scaled graphs (1:2) from M2 while introducing the key M3 concept: scale of 5 is more efficient for larger numbers. Students collect personal data, then OBSERVE the same data displayed as BOTH 1:2 and 1:5 picture graphs side-by-side. The discovery that 'scale of 5 uses fewer symbols' creates curiosity and sets up Lesson's formalization of the skip-counting connection. Key difference from M2: grouping animation moves to Lesson Section 1; Warmup focuses on efficiency discovery.

variables.format: Data Collection Game → Side-by-Side Scale Comparison → Efficiency Discovery → Bridge

interactions: W.1: Data Collection Game — Mario Party-style counting, 3 categories, multiples of 5 only (10-30 range)
W.2: Scale Comparison Display — same student data shown as 1:2 AND 1:5 picture graphs side-by-side (both vertical, keys visible); MC asks what student notices about the two graphs
W.3: Efficiency Discovery — quantify the difference; 'Which scale is more efficient for showing [X] items?' MC: Scale of 2 / Scale of 5
W.4: Bridge to Lesson — scale-of-5 graph remains; skip-counting teased but not taught; 'Do you remember how to count by 5s?'

phases.purpose: Formalize scale of 5 and explicitly connect to skip-counting by 5s. Students create picture graphs with scaffolding (1 of 4 categories pre-filled from animation). Build on Warmup's scale comparison discovery. Grouping Animation moves here from Warmup — shows items grouping into 5s, then becoming symbols.
phases.interaction_count: 6 (1.6 has 2 prompts = counts as 2 per program structure)
interactions: 1.1: Building on Scale Comparison — brief callback to Warmup side-by-side; MC: 'What does each symbol represent when the scale is 5?' (distractors: 2, 10, 1)
1.2: Grouping Animation — 'Favorite Playground Activities'; 20 items → 4 groups of 5 → 4 symbols; Guide counts aloud '5, 10, 15, 20'; observation only (no student prompt)
1.3: Skip-Counting Connection — new graph; 'Count by 5s to check: 4 symbols = how many?' MC includes symbol-count distractor (4) and addition distractor (9)
1.4: Your Turn — Guided Creation — Slides has 15; student sets height at 3 symbols; 1 category pre-filled (Swings from animation)
1.5: Complete the Graph — Monkey Bars has 25; student sets height at 5 symbols
1.5b: Continue Building — Sandbox has 10; student sets height at 2 symbols
1.6: Reading Your Graph — Most and Least (two-prompt interaction; no data table): click most popular category, then click fewest

phases.purpose: Introduce bar graph CREATION (students read bar graphs in M1, but creating is new). Same scale (5), new skill. Students see that bars and symbols show the same information. Axis vocabulary introduced. Students create bar graphs with scaffolding (2 of 4-5 categories pre-filled).
interactions: 2.1: Two Ways to Show Scale of 5 — completed picture graph alongside pre-filled bar graph (same data); data table visible; 'Where do you see the 5s on the bar graph?' introduces axis vocabulary
2.2: Reading Bar Height — highlight Slides on both graphs; 'What's the HEIGHT of the Slides bar?' MC includes symbol-count distractor (3)
2.3: Reading the Axis — Scale of 5 — new 5-category context; axis highlighted; 'What comes after 20 on this axis?' MC: 21, 25, 30, 22
2.4: Your Turn — Create a Bar — set Pizza bar height to 20; click-to-height interaction
2.4b: Complete the Bar Graph — finish remaining 2 bars (Salad=15, Pasta=25)
2.5: Reading from Completed Bar Graph — picture graph shown alongside for comparison; 'How many symbols should picture graph have for Pasta (25)?' connects formats
2.6: Comparison Question — Bars Work Too — 'How many MORE chose Tacos than Salad?' MC includes sum distractor (45 = Misconception #6)

phases.purpose: Remove scaffolding. Students create full graphs from numeric data tables without animation. Larger values (25-40 range). Students demonstrate they can work with BOTH formats independently. Horizontal orientation proves flexibility. Three-scale toggle (3.2b) extends M2's binary comparison to a three-way pattern.
interactions: 3.1: Picture Graph — No Scaffolding (Horizontal) — Guide explicitly acknowledges orientation switch; 'Books Read This Month' 4 categories; student sets Liam's row first (35 books = 7 symbols)
3.2: Complete Picture Graph — fill remaining 3 rows (Emma=4, Noah=5, Olivia=8); data table visible
3.2a: Comparison Question — 'How many FEWER books did Emma read than Olivia?' from completed horizontal graph (no data table); MC includes sum distractor (60)

3.2b: Three-Scale Toggle (Observation Only) — student toggles between scales 1, 2, 5 on their own graph; guided sequence 5→1→2→5; Guide highlights Olivia (largest value) to show 8→40→20→8 symbols; MC: which scale uses fewest? Data table visible as stable anchor
3.3: Reading the Axis — Horizontal — new context 'Screen Time'; axis along bottom; 'What comes after 35?' MC: 36, 40, 45, 30
3.3b: Bar Graph — Independent Creation (Horizontal) — create all 4 bars from data table (no scaffolding)
3.4: Reading for Totals — 'Saturday and Sunday IN ALL?' from completed bar graph (no data table); MC includes subtraction distractor (5)
3.5: Final Check — What's the Same? — both graphs visible; 'What do picture graphs and bar graphs have in common?' MC: 'They both use the same scale'

phases.purpose: Verify Lesson understanding before Practice. Tests picture graph creation with scale of 5 only. Bar graph creation is assessed in M4. Focus: can student calculate symbol counts via skip-counting, create a scaled picture graph from a data table, and read values from completed graphs?

graph_types: picture graph only (bar graphs assessed in M4)

interactions: EC.1: Symbol Count Calculation (IDENTIFY) — 'Favorite Drinks' context; 'Juice has 25 votes, how many symbols?' MC: 4, 5, 25, 30 (distractors catch Misconceptions #1 and #2); tests Lesson 1.3-1.5
EC.2: Create Picture Graph (CREATE) — complete Milk and Water columns (2 of 4 pre-filled); click-to-height; tests Lesson 1.4-1.5 and 3.1-3.2
EC.3: Read Total Value (IDENTIFY) — completed graph; data table hidden; 'How many chose Water?' MC: 6, 25, 30, 35 (symbol-count distractor: 6); tests Lesson 1.6 and 2.1-2.2

phases.purpose: Connect scale of 5 to real-world contexts and consolidate that students now have multiple scales in their toolkit. Intentionally lean (3 interactions) appropriate for M3's thinner conceptual depth vs M2. Focus: real-world connection and 'two scales in toolkit' framing. NOT another scale efficiency comparison (covered in M2) and NOT format comparison (covered in M1).

task_types: Type C: Real-World Bridge (5s are everywhere)

interactions: S.1: Real-World Bridge — four images (hand, tally marks, nickels, clock); 'Which of these uses groups of 5? Select all that apply.' Correct: all four; connects grouping-by-5s to familiar contexts

S.2: Metacognitive Reflection — simple display showing 'Scale of 2: Each symbol = 2' and 'Scale of 5: Each symbol = 5'; 'If you had to show 30 items, which scale needs fewer symbols?' MC: Scale of 2 / Scale of 5 / same; 'You're building a toolkit.'

S.3: Identity-Building Closure — representative picture and bar graphs from Lesson; 'You started with scale of 2. Now scale of 5 too. Two scales, two formats.'; Bridge: 'You started with scale of 1, then 2, now 5. Next time, we'll add scale of 10 to your toolkit.'
